data.title: Term And Repo In Vogue In U.S. Money Markets
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA

Money market funds remain a valid investment choice, even if the Fed cuts some more

Money market funds have seen a seemingly relentless rise in assets under management in the past five or six years. The first phase of this occurred during the first year or so of the pandemic, bolstered by corporates beefing up cash buffers. On some measures, the proportion of institutional money market funds held by corporates rose from sub-40% to more than 60%. It has since settled in the 50-60% area. The second phase coincided with the Fed’s rate hike cycle, as absolute rates rose. And as money market exposure is 'price risk-free', it has offered an attractive foil to bond or risk asset alternatives. Even with the Fed cutting rates, absolute rates in the 4.25%+ area remain attractive.

For 2025, there will be a tolerance for a few more cuts from the Fed before players begin to question the absolute levels attainable. A 4%+ handle remains very attractive. A break below 4% would not necessarily cause severe liquidation. We suspect such conversations will begin if the Fed gets below 3%, as we see the 3% area as a neutral valuation for the funds rate (so rates below that should be viewed as sub-optimal). That said, corporates have evolved into large holders of money market funds, and such holdings are liable to prove quite sticky. We'll see the US$7tr area held for the foreseeable, as the Fed is unlikely to get anywhere near the 3% area.

Money market fund holdings outright, and as a proportion of GDP

As a percentage of the value of GDP, money market fund holdings were even higher before the Great Financial Crisis. Several regulatory changes since then saw holdings fall from a high of 27% of GDP in 2008 to a low of sub-14% of GDP some 10 years later. Then the pandemic hit. And now money market funds are back up to a little over 23% of GDP. Looking ahead, it is more likely that money market funds trend in line with GDP, rather than faster than GDP.

Breakout of funds by investor type and product shows some stark individual movements

Institutional players have edged their Prime Fund holding lower post-recent rule changes. But this has been offset by ongoing significant rises in money market investment by retail players. Additional institutional cash is predominantly biased toward Government Funds where liquidity requirements are higher and "gates and fees" are less of an issue (compared to Prime funds).

Institutional cash still biased to Government Funds, while retail growth is in Prime Funds

In terms of portfolio breakdown, Government Funds are seeing an ongoing rise in holdings of Treasury Debt (effectively bills). In the wider market setting, Bills now represent some 22% of overall government debt financing, up from the 15% area in 2022/23, implying that extra Bills issuance is being employed to take pressure off coupon issuance.

Exposure to Repo is steadier now in Government Funds, but still well down from the highs seen in 2023. The preferred value play here has been in Bills. For now, the high net Bills issuance environment remains in play. However, as we get deeper into debt ceiling control measures there will be more pressure to reduce net Bills issuance, which can add a premium to prices. That can result in more cash moving back into Repo.

In Prime Funds, Repo (reflecting equity repo) exposures remain on the increase, as is exposure to commercial paper (CP), while certificates of deposit (CD) and ordinary deposits have been steadier to a tad lower. This is in part correlated to the quantitative tightening (QT) process.

Big moves in holdings of Repo vs Bills in Government Funds, and CP plus Equity Repo in Prime Funds

As shown in the chart above, the breakdown of money market fund portfolio holdings shows Treasuries, Agency debt and Repo dominate in Government Funds. Most of the rest is in commercial paper and certificates of deposit (in Prime Funds).

Breakdown of attainable rates and spread to the Fed's reverse repo facility (RRP) rate

In terms of attainable rates, the entire spectrum of overnight CP rates has shifted to comfortably above the Fed’s reverse repo rate, which itself was moved down 5bp to the funds rate floor at the December FOMC meeting. This reduces the attractiveness of the Fed’s reverse repo facility, and should increase the relative attractiveness of market Repo.

At the same time, CP spreads are not yet quite as wide as they were during the pre-pandemic years, but they are edging towards these types of levels, as shown in the chart below.

Spectrum of Overnight CP spreads versus the Fed's RRP – some widening of late...

Repo is liable to prove more attractive looking ahead. The relevance of the Fed’s reverse repo facility has been downsized significantly from a relative value perspective, as better Repo terms are now typically attainable on the private market. Increasingly we’d see the reverse repo window accessed around quarter (and month) ends when the Fed’s facility is used to get past gaps left by counterparties needing to window dress for regulatory reasons.

GC presenting better value versus RRP, and AA Financial CP better still

The shift in the Federal Reserve's policy narrative is extending the term on the commercial paper (CP) curve, reversing the inversion that occurred when the Fed started its rate-cutting agenda. Still, three-month AA Financial CP is trading broadly flat to overnight rates. In the A2/P2 nonfinancial and AA-asset backed the evolution of some terms is more pronounced.

With the Fed still technically in cutting mode, terms will tend to be contained. While we'll need to get to the end of the rate-cutting cycle with some degree of certainty before terms can really open up, there is still value in terming out as a means to locking in current levels of rates. This will prove to be a good play should the Fed cut rates later in the year (we think there'll be two more 25bp cuts) in the second half of 2025 (September and December meetings).

Commercial paper term from O/N to 3mth has dis-inverted, and more to go ahead...

Reserves set to hit a notional floor at around $3tr by mid-year, necessitating an end to QT

Having peaked at around US$2.5tr at the end of 2022, the volumes going into the Fed’s reverse repo facility have persistently fallen, now at below US$100bn. Our baseline view is for this to settle much closer to zero. We continue to view such balances going back to the Fed as a manifestation of excess liquidity, which should broadly evaporate by mid-2025. From here, reductions in excess liquidity due to quantitative tightening (QT) are more likely to be felt through falls in Bank Reserves.

The Fed needs to be careful here as in 2019 the QT process pushed Bank Reserves down to the US$1.5tr area, which caused some severe tightness. Back then, the value of US GDP was around US$20tr. So, Bank Reserves hit around 7.5% of GDP. The thinking ahead is this needs to be closer to 10% of GDP. With the value of US GDP running at around US$30tr, that implies a floor for Bank Reserves at around US$3tr.

Fed's reverse repo volumes are practically at zero, while overall reserves edging towards the notional $3tr floor

Currently, Bank Reserves are running at some US$3.3tr. Adding the cushion of almost US$100bn at the Fed’s reverse repo facility, there is an overall "excess" liquidity balance of some $3.4tr. Meanwhile, QT is running at around US$60bn per month and acts to reduce excess liquidity at that pace. Based on this, within the coming half year, the US$3tr Bank Reserves floor will be hit. Consequently, the Fed will likely bring its QT programme to a halt by the middle of the year.

A complicating factor right now is the debt ceiling. This was automatically re-instated on 2 January 2025, and the biggest implication of this for liquidity circumstances comes from the requirement for the US Treasury to spend down its cash balances (as a cushion for the inability to engage in higher net issuance). Currently, the Treasury has a cash balance of around US$840bn. As we progress towards the middle of the year, the US Treasury will be in payout mode, and in that sense will tend to bolster Bank Reserves.

US Treasury has built a decent cash reserve that it can ease lower should an early summer debt ceiling saga unfold

It is, however, probable that net bills issuance is an area that could see some shrinkage, certainly as debt ceiling management intensifies (by early summer at the latest), assuming no adjustment to coupon issuance sizes. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has indeed intimated that coupon issuance is set to remain broadly unchanged in the coming quarters.

For now, the increased net issuance of bills has added value to that sector. However, this value may diminish if bill issuance needs to be temporarily reduced. The debt ceiling issue could also be efficiently proactively resolved by Congress, but that remains to be seen.

Bottom line, there is net opportunity in this space

Looking through the various pushes and pulls, there are a few key takeaways. First, the absolute rate attainable in the money market funds space remains attractive and will remain so even if the Fed cuts some more.

Money curves have generally dis-inverted, which generates an opportunity to term out where feasible, to get today's rates (or at least close to them) for longer.

The Fed's RRP facility will increasingly be used just at turns, broadly ending routine usage. Market repo is more attractive here in relative value terms.

As QT comes to an end by mid-year, idle liquidity is expected to decrease, potentially pushing generic money market rates higher. Although these changes are marginal, they create a more natural environment compared to the peak period in 2022-23, when around $2.5tr was directed to the Fed's reverse repo facility.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Valmont Industries, Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Valmont Industries, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Wake Up, It's Time For A Business Plan - Reiterating Buy On Upwork
data.description:
Upwork Inc. (NASDAQ:UPWK) reported Q4 earnings Wednesday after the bell, beating on revenue, EPS, and optimistic guidance for Q1 of 2025. When I first wrote on Upwork, I initiated with a buy based on my view that cost reduction, increased scale, and platform improvements would boost profitability and position the company to outperform.

Now that the earnings results are out, I can say it played out decently well; Q4 revenue is up 4% year-over-year to an all-time high of $191.5 million, beating the expected $181.2 million. The beat on revenue was mainly driven by better-than-expected results from enterprise clients. This confirms my thoughts on the Business Plus plan acting as a jumping point for scalability, and I think this will be a theme that continues to boost the company's financials going forward. Management's diligent cost reduction strategy also worked in their favor, with Q4 profit margin, as shown in the chart below, up a massive 68% year-over-year to 77% (and don't we all love high profitability). And on platform improvements (mostly around AI), GSV, or gross sales value, from AI grew 60% year-over-year in FY 2024, continuing the AI-related growth we saw in Q3 into the fourth quarter. As for guidance, Upwork estimates Q1 revenue with a midpoint of $188.5 million, ahead of analyst consensus of $183.56 million.

Although the stock price has been pretty much flat since my last note, in line with the S&P 500 (SP500), I find that the catalysts I discussed then still apply now but are taking longer to play out. Hence, I expect more upside in the near to mid-term as Upwork maintains its profitability strategy and continues scaling its Business Plus plan for enterprises.

The Business Of Enterprise

Not to beat a dead horse, but I really see enterprise as the growth catalyst for Upwork in 2025. Enterprises aren't new here, but management's hyper-focus on them is. This quarter's numbers point to a lot more engagement, with “GSV per active enterprise account growing year-over-year for the first time in recent quarters,” as noted on the call.

In Q4, Upwork closed 21 enterprise deals and grew managed services, which cater to those large enterprises, by 8% year-over-year. Enterprise revenue reached $27.8 million in Q4, up 5% year-over-year, and $107.2 million in FY 2024, up 4% from FY 2023 – that's 14% of total revenue in the full year. This is all the more impressive considering that we're seeing this momentum against a challenging macro backdrop where most enterprises are cutting budgets. Even in the face of tougher economic conditions, Upwork still proved to navigate the storm better than the industry, with FY 2024 revenue up 12% year-over-year versus the staffing industry, up 9%. I think momentum here will continue to be supported by Upwork's Business Plus plan, which was launched in October. It's basically a premium plan that makes the entire process of connecting Upwork's current marketplace and enterprise offerings smoother, aka making adoption way easier. For this quarter, the company acquired +1,000 active users on Business Plus since its launch. Now, I think this isn't showing up in GSV growth yet, but it will with time and a healthier macroeconomic environment.

Speaking of GSV, in my last article on Upwork, which was post-Q3 earnings, I touched on the GSV decline and what I think it means to Upwork. I expected GSV to keep declining in the near term as the company shifts to higher-paying enterprise clients. This quarter, unsurprisingly, GSV was down another 3.6%. Still, I don't see this as a major point of concern because while fewer, higher-paying jobs may reflect negatively on the GSV and take rate in the near term, these are what I'd call growing pains. Some more near-term GSV pressure is a small price to pay for a higher and more resilient GSV trend against future macro uncertainty.

Valuation

Upwork has a market cap of $2.07 billion and an enterprise value of $1.82 billion. Its beta, 1.57, shows somewhat high volatility in the stock. Forward P/E of 13.09 is slightly lower than the sector median of 19.97, indicating an undervalued stock. EV/sales and price-to-book, at 2.40 and 3.97, respectively, are both slightly above the sector medians of 1.96 and 3.09, respectively.

Profitability, as discussed above, is far ahead of the sector median of 32%. Stock momentum, up 67% over the last 6 months, is also significantly outperforming the sector's 4%. Revisions on EPS and revenue are promising, with eight upward revisions on EPS and ten on revenue in the past three months, versus one downward revision on each. According to data from Refinitiv, 18.2% of Street Analysts give the stock a strong buy, 54.5% give it a buy, and 27.3% give it a hold, with the rest giving it a sell.

The price targets for Upwork increased slightly over the last 90 days, with the PT median up from $18 in mid-November to $19 in mid-February and the PT mean up from $17.64 to $19.36 in the same time frame. The slow increase tells me that investor confidence hasn't really kicked in yet, but I say it's better to be positioned in the stock before that happens.

What makes me think Upwork is attractive at its current stock price is the fact that the stock is trading lower than the S&P 500 over the past month, highlighting more upside potential considering the positives on the table. Upwork is down 4.4%, trailing the S&P 500 up 2%, as shown below.

What's Next?

For the coming quarters, I'll be keeping an eye on the Business Plan performance, the GSV growth, and the (so far impressive) profit margin growth. I think that Upwork will continue to demonstrate strong stock momentum and high profitability with management's swift and disciplined execution, especially when it comes to the enterprise segment. The market may not quite see the light at the end of the tunnel for Upwork yet, but considering the potential upsides, this makes an even better case for jumping into the stock while it's still dark.

data.title: Sterling Infrastructure's Fall From Grace Is Warranted - Still No Margin Of Safety
data.description:
STRL's Oversold Status Is Warranted - More Pain Ahead

We previously covered Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (NASDAQ:STRL) in September 2024, discussing its promising high-growth prospects thanks to the ongoing infrastructure boom, particularly in data centers and e-commerce distribution centers through Meta (META) and Amazon (AMZN).

Despite the robust financial performance and raised FY2024 guidance, it was apparent that there was a minimal margin of safety then, attributed to the expensive valuations, pulled forward upside potential, and elevated short interest, resulting in our reiterated Hold rating then.

STRL 1Y Stock Price

Since then, STRL has continued to chart new peaks at $206s ranges, prior to the DeepSeek induced market sell-off on January 27, 2025 and the stock's subsequent underperformance then.

Without delving into over much detail into the well covered topic, the China released DeepSeek LLM was reportedly trained “at a much lower cost” of $5.6M, compared to those of Alphabet (GOOG) DeepMind Gemini's at approximately $191M and Microsoft's (MSFT) partner, OpenAI ChatGPT-4 at $78M.

Combined with the LLM's on par performances to those released by the US Big Tech peers, it was unsurprising that the wider stock market had experienced a painful one-day selloff with over $1T in market cap erased, STRL included.

Given STRL's stock price underperformance since then, it is apparent that market sentiments remain mixed despite the promising AI/ data center related spending trends highlighted by numerous market leaders, including Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOG) in their recent earnings call.

Most importantly, two of STRL's key customers, Amazon (AMZN) and Meta (META), have already guided robust FY2025 capex spending of $105.2B (+26.7% YoY/ +523.9% from FY2019 levels of $16.86B) and $62.5B (+59.3% YoY/ +313.9% from FY2019 levels of $15.1B), respectively, with it triggering great insights into the former's intermediate term prospects.

The same raised FY2024 outlook has also been offered by the STRL management in the last earnings call, with the construction company expecting to report revenues of $2.16B at the midpoint (+9.6% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 14.4% (+1.3 points YoY), adj EPS of $5.92 (+32.4% YoY).

This is compared to the original guidance of $2.17B (+10.1% YoY), 13.4% (+0.3 points YoY), and $5.00 (+11.8% YoY) offered in the FQ4'23 earnings call, respectively, with it underscoring why the stock's oversold status may have been painful.

Despite the mixed top-line performance over the past few quarters, STRL has continued to deliver outsized bottom-line expansions with it highlighting our optimism surrounding its potential bottom-line beat in the upcoming FQ4'24 earnings call on February 25, 2025.

If anything, readers may want to note that the US President Trump recently announced the Stargate Project, a "$500B, four-year initiative to build out artificial intelligence infrastructure," with "10 data centers for the project were already under construction in Texas, and that more were planned."

This development may trigger more tailwinds for construction companies indeed, STRL included, as similarly observed in its stable backlog at $2.06B as of FQ3'24 (-1.9% QoQ/ +2.4% YoY) and near-term opportunities (unsigned backlog) at $0.31B (-11.4% QoQ/ -18.4% YoY).

This is especially since that "data center-related revenue increased approximately 90% in the quarter and now represents over 50% of segment backlog," especially aided by the ongoing transition of its business profile to "large, multi-phase projects" with "a pipeline of high probability future phase work that now totals over half a billion dollars."

If anything, we are already seeing an excellent expansion in STRL's gross profit margins to 21.9% (+2.6 points QoQ/ +5.5 YoY/ +12.3 from FY2019 levels of 9.6%) and adj EBITDA margins to 16.9% (+2 points QoQ/ +4.2 YoY/ +11.3 from FY2019 levels of 5.6%), with it underscoring the management's stellar execution despite the challenging macroeconomic environment over the past few years.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

The same promising numbers have also been observed in the consensus forward estimates, with STRL expected to generate a top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +6.1%/ +17.5% through FY2026.

This is compared to the original estimates of +8.9%/ +13.2%, with the accelerating bottom-line growth attributed to the higher margin data center/ manufacturing end markets well balancing the decelerating top-line growth observed in the residential end market softness.

STRL Valuations

This reason is likely why the STRL stock looks a lot more reasonable at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 22.20x and the PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.06x, down from the prior article levels of 26.20x/ 1.75x and the recent heights of 32.48x/ 1.85x in January 2025, respectively, despite the notable upgrade from the 5Y mean of 14.21x/ 0.38x.

STRL Peer Group

The same conclusion may also be derived when comparing to the sector median PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.83x and its direct peer group mean of 1.12x (aside from MYRG at 8.74x), with it underscoring why STRL's recent pullback has been much needed.

So, Is STRL Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

STRL 2Y Stock Price

For now, STRL has already lost -35.7% or the equivalent -$2.26B of its market capitalization since the 52 week peak of $206.07, with the stock also breaching numerous support levels while trading way below the 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages.

Unfortunately, it is painfully apparent that the stock remains inflated compared to our prior fair value estimate of $70.70 offered in our last article, based on the management's prior FY2024 adj EPS guidance of $5.67 (+26.8% YoY) and the 5Y P/E mean of 12.48x (nearer to its peers).

The same may be observed in our updated fair value estimates of $84.10, based on STRL's raised FY2025 guidance of $5.92 (+32.4% YoY) and the updated 5Y P/E mean of 14.21x.

Based on the consensus raised FY2026 adj EPS estimates of $7.25, there remains a minimal margin of safety to our updated long-term price target of $103 as well, with it perhaps underscoring why the stock has yet to find a floor despite the massive pullback.

Combined with the higher short interest volume by +13.2% on a YoY basis/ +341.3% over the past two years and the stock's lower highs/ lower lows thus far, we believe that STRL is likely to remain volatile moving forward, resulting in our reiterated Hold rating here.

Interested investors may be better off tuning into the upcoming earnings call and observing the stock price movement for a little longer.

data.title: Palladyne AI: Revolutionizing Robotics With AI
data.description:
Authored by Garvit Bhandari

Investment summary

Palladyne (NASDAQ:PDYN) is a leading provider of AI/ML software platforms for various robotic applications, with its two software products, Palladyne IQ and Palladyne Pilot. The growth prospects for these two products remain bright given their applications across a wide range of industries, and the market potential for robotics software overall. Moreover, the company’s recent agreement with Red Cat is a big boost for Palladyne Pilot. All in all, I have a Strong Buy rating on the stock with a target price of $15.

Two software products nearing commercialization

PDYN has two software products nearing commercialization – first, Palladyne IQ focused on stationary industrial robots and cobots, and second, Palladyne Pilot catering to mobile robotic platforms such as drones and unmanned ground vehicles.

For Palladyne IQ, PDYN released its initial commercial version in October 2024 as it completed Phase 1 of a multi-million dollar contract. The company is conducting customer trials for Palladyne IQ and expects to be generating revenues from commercial customers in 2025.

Also, PDYN expects to release its initial commercial version for Palladyne Pilot by the end of 1Q25. For Palladyne Pilot, the company has completed a customer trial for the early version of the product.

The company has revenue-generating contracts from the US government for both the products and has met all the development milestones associated with the contracts. As such, the company is well on track to launch commercial versions of its products.

Potential use cases across diverse industries

PDYN’s two software products have a wide range of applications across diverse industries. Palladyne IQ is useful in several applications such as manufacturing, kitting and part sequencing and surface preparation, while Palladyne Pilot is focused on defense and commercial applications.

For manufacturing, the expected benefits are: lower costs, quick ability for robots to perform new task, minimum downtime for new task training, running assembly lines with mixed products to meet demand, and extension of usability and life of robots.

For kitting applications, Palladyne IQ helps in picking/placing/sorting of parts into assembly kits or containers and helps achieve continuous workflow without disruptions.

Similarly, for surface preparation applications, the product helps in various tasks such as the removal of paint, rust, and debris from surfaces and cleans and prepares the surface for maintenance or finishing processes.

On the other hand, Palladyne Pilot has applications in unmanned aerial vehicles. The key benefits offered are: persistent sensor-based detection, tracking, and classification that enhances situational awareness and enhances the collective knowledge and understanding of the entire fleet.

Given such a wide range of potential use cases, the company’s commercialization prospects remain bright.

Partnership with Red Cat: A boost for Palladyne Pilot

PDYN’s partnership with Red Cat is a big boost for its Palladyne Pilot product.

In October 2024, PDYN and Red Cat announced a partnership to add Palladyne AI’s artificial intelligence software into Red Cat’s Teal drones.

Palladyne Pilot is expected to be available on all Teal drones and will be included in new drones shipped to customers who desire the features and functionality provided by the platform.

Red Cat recently announced it was selected as the winner of the U.S. Army’s Short Range Reconnaissance (SRR) Program of Record after a rigorous test and evaluation process for its Teal next-generation sUAS, designated as the Black Widow and WEB.

The partnership with Red Cat is progressing well, and the company recently announced the completion of the first successful flight with multiple Teal drones embedded with Palladyne Pilot AI software.

Recent development

In January 2025, the company was awarded a contract from the Air Force Research Laboratory to migrate Palladyne Pilot AI software platform to next-generation US-made AI computing chips. The project duration is 26 months, beginning in early 2025.

Financial projections

Based on the 9M24 results, I estimate FY24 sales at $9.4 million. I have assumed YoY growth of 25% and 50% for FY25 and FY26 respectively, based on the expectation of commercialization of its software products.

Moreover, given this topline growth, I estimate EBITDA margins to improve from (255)% in FY24 to (125)% in FY26.

Valuation

I am valuing PDYN based on the P/S relative valuation.

I have compared PDYN with some of its industry peers. I value PDYN using a target P/S multiple of 25.1x. The multiple is at a significant premium to the peer group average given that PDYN is growing revenues at ~22% for the next two years vs. ~9% for the peer group average.

Based on 25.1x FY26 Sales of ~$18 million, the target price (TP) stands at $15.

Risks

Below are the main risks to my investment thesis:

  • The company’s products are nearing commercialization, and any delay in the launch of its products could have an adverse impact on the company's results.
  • The industry where PDYN operates is highly competitive, and the company faces stiff competition from several large and established players.
  • The company caters to government customers and is subject to several risks such as contract delays, budgets, disputes, and litigation.

Conclusion

PDYN is a leading provider of AI/ML software for various robotics applications. The company has recently released the commercial version of Palladyne IQ and expects to launch Palladyne Pilot shortly. The company’s products have a wide range of applications across various industries. I believe that the recent agreement with Red Cat is a big boost for Palladyne Pilot products.

Over the last year, this stock has skyrocketed by more than 2000%. Still, I believe this AI stock will continue to perform well. Summarily, I have a Buy rating on the stock with a target price of $15.

data.title: Retail Exuberance Sets Market Up For A Correction
data.description:
Last week, we discussed the surge in retail exuberance in the market following the election of President Trump.

“The market defies more negative news because retail investors continue to step in and “buy the dip.” In our recent Bull Bear reports, we discussed the push by retail investors, but looking at retail sentiment is quite remarkable. Since the pandemic, retail investors have never been this bullish on the stock market. Such is amazing, given that their mailboxes are not being stuffed with government stimulus checks.”

Retail investors are expected to become more bullish about increasing equity exposure when markets rise. However, the more extended markets become, the more confident investors are about increasing speculative risk. In other words, “success breeds confidence.” One way we can view retail exuberance is through the use of leverage. As investors become increasingly confident about higher stock prices in the future, they are willing to borrow money to take on more risk. We see this by comparing investor confidence in higher stock prices to the annual rate of change in margin debt.

The “Fear Of Missing Out” or “F.O.M.O.” is a compelling motivation for retail investors to chase stocks higher. This is reflected in the current household equity allocations record level, which explains the market’s high valuation levels.

As Howard Marks noted in a December 2020 Bloomberg interview:

“Fear of missing out has taken over from the fear of losing money. If people are risk-tolerant and afraid of being out of the market, they buy aggressively, in which case you can’t find any bargains. That’s where we are now. That’s what the Fed engineered by putting rates at zero…we are back to where we were a year ago—uncertainty, prospective returns that are even lower than they were a year ago, and higher asset prices than a year ago. People are back to having to take on more risk to get return. At Oaktree, we are back to a cautious approach. This is not the kind of environment in which you would be buying with both hands.

The prospective returns are low on everything.”

As shown, Howard was eventually right. In 2022, the decline wiped out all of the previous year’s gains and then some. However, this is a critical point about margin debt and leverage in general.

Margin debt represents the amount of speculation occurring in the market. In other words, margin debt is the “gasoline,” which drives markets higher as the leverage provides for the additional purchasing power of assets. However, leverage also works in reverse, as it supplies the accelerant for more significant declines as lenders “force” the sale of assets to cover credit lines without regard to the borrower’s position.

The last sentence is the most important. The issue with margin debt is that the unwinding of leverage is not at the investor’s discretion. That process is at the discretion of the broker-dealers that extended that leverage in the first place. (In other words, if you don’t sell to cover, the broker-dealer will do it for you.) When lenders fear they may not recoup their credit lines, they force the borrower to put in more cash or sell assets to cover the debt. The problem is that “margin calls” generally happen simultaneously, as falling asset prices impact all lenders simultaneously.

Margin debt is not an issue – until it is.

So, where are we currently?

Margin Debt Confirms The Exuberance

We have noted recently that retail exuberance is evident as they take on leverage through measures not recorded by broker-dealer margin levels. Whether it is through a surge in options trading or through leveraged ETFs, investors have figured out ways to increase their risk/return profile.

However, when we specifically look at margin debt, a loan against underlying collateral in brokerage accounts, those debt levels have surged to a record. As shown above, the year-over-year rate of change in debt is rising sharply but certainly can go further if retail exuberance continues.

However, look at the red line, “free cash balances.” As noted, margin debt supports the advance when markets rise, as investors can deploy additional leverage to increase buying power. Therefore, the recent rise in margin debt is unsurprising as investor exuberance climbs. The chart below shows the relationship between cash balances and the market. I have inverted free cash balances, so the relationship between increases in margin debt and the market is better represented. (Free cash balances are the difference between margin balances less cash and credit balances in margin accounts.).

Note that during the 1987 correction, the 2015-2016 “Brexit/Taper Tantrum,” the 2018 “Rate Hike Mistake,” and the “COVID Dip,” the market never broke its uptrend, and cash balances never turned positive. Both a break of the rising bullish trend and positive free cash balances were the 2000 and 2008 bear market hallmarks. With negative cash balances at another all-time high, the next downturn could be another “correction.” However, if, or when, the long-term bullish trend is broken, the unwinding of margin debt will add “fuel to the fire.”

As noted, rising leverage is the “fuel” for bull market advances. What makes rising margin debt levels more dangerous is when retail exuberance feeds into all risk assets simultaneously.

All Correlations Go To One

An old saying states that “what goes up must come down.” During speculative market frenzies, investors rush to allocate risk to rising prices. As prices increase, investors become less concerned about underlying risk and begin to rationalize why “this time is different.” However, whatever the rationalization is, prices rise due to one simplistic factor: supply and demand. In the current market, there is just “too much money and leverage, chasing too few assets.”

We have seen that inherent risk play out in previous history. This is particularly the case when correlations between risk assets rise to more extreme levels. For instance, as shown, the correlation between the S&P 500 index and emerging and international markets is near one. (1.00 is a perfectly positive correlation, and -1.00 is a perfectly negative non-correlation.)

There are many narratives surrounding Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies in general. However, due to its price volatility, Bitcoin has been an effective “leveraged” bet on rising asset markets.

Interestingly, gold, while often considered a hedge for inflation, has also seen a sharp increase in its correlation to equity prices. Historically, when the correlation of gold to the S&P 500 index reaches 1.00, such has been a peak in gold prices. However, except for the late '90s, the peak correlation between gold and the S&P 500 was also near the peaks of financial markets. Such is because the speculative frenzy to chase all assets simultaneously undermined the value of portfolio diversification. As such, when markets eventually cracked, and leverage was unwound, all assets were sold simultaneously.

Conclusion

While the immediate response to this analysis will be, “But Lance, margin debt is only slightly higher than in 2021,” there are many differences between then and today. The lack of stimulus payments, zero interest rates, and $120 billion in monthly “Quantitative Easing” are just a few. However, some glaring similarities exist, including the surge in negative cash balances and extreme deviations from long-term means.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Truxton Corporation: Too Early For A Downgrade
data.description:
Back in November of last year, one company that I took a bullish stance on was Truxton Corporation (OTCPK:TRUX). This is a rather small bank with a market capitalization as of this writing of about $218 million. This isn't the smallest player in the space that I have analyzed, but it is far from the largest. Personally, I think that digging into smaller firms is generally more interesting than larger ones. This is because they are often overlooked by the broader investment community and might offer greater upside as a result. Back then, I remember being impressed with the overall high quality of the institution from an asset perspective. The stock wasn't exactly the cheapest. But shares were just cheap enough to justify a ‘buy’ rating.

Since that time, we have seen data come in for one additional fiscal quarter. During that time, the company has demonstrated continued growth on both its top and bottom lines. Its asset quality remains high and shares are actually quite attractive, relatively speaking, compared to other similar institutions. The stock has experienced some nice upside because of this as well. Shares are currently up 6.6% at a time when the S&P 500 is up only 1.2%. Even though the stock might not be the most affordable on the market, I believe that this kind of outperformance will probably continue for some time. Because of this, I believe that keeping the company rated a ‘buy’ makes the most sense at this point.

Taking a second look at Truxton Corporation

During the most recent quarter, which would be the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year, Truxton Corporation reported deposits of $866.4 million. Interestingly, this actually represents a decline from the $888.8 million the institution had during the third quarter. However, it is up soundly from the $782 million the business ended 2023 with. In general, I'm always concerned when I see some sort of drop in deposits. Unfortunately, we don't really have detail as to why this decline occurred.

All management said was that they continue to fund their growth from a single banking location. The fact that this statement was made in the exact same bullet point as the discussion about the drop in deposits suggests to me that this really is just an example of how volatile results can become for such a small business. Even one or two major clients can cause a big swing in overall assets and liabilities.

When it comes to other parts of the balance sheet, we have seen increases. Loans, for instance, inched up from $658.3 million to $663.5 million. It's also worth pointing out that only $11,000 worth of these loans are considered non-performing. This is pretty solid if I do say so myself. From the end of the 2023 fiscal year to the present day, loans increased further, climbing from $651.5 million to $663.5 million. But of course, there are other aspects of the balance sheet that deserve attention as well.

One area that has seen some decline has been securities. After jumping from $259.9 million at the end of 2023 to $295.5 million in the third quarter of last year, there was a subsequent decline to $258.3 million at the end of 2024. Cash and cash equivalents remained virtually flat, falling from $35 million in the third quarter of the year to $34 million today. But both of those represent increases compared to the $9.2 million the company ended 2023 with. Debt, meanwhile, came in at $22.7 million as of the end of the most recent quarter. That compares to the $72.6 million the institution had at the end of 2024.

All things considered, these mixed results on the balance sheet aren't great, but they aren't awful. What is more impressive, however, is the income statement. Even though the company's net interest margin dipped ever so slightly from 2.78% in the final quarter of 2023 to 2.77% in the final quarter of 2024, the firm’s net interest income happened to rise from $6.3 million to $6.7 million. This improvement stems largely from a decline in the cost of deposits from $7.7 million to $6.8 million and from a drop in short-term borrowing costs from $0.3 million to $0.1 million.

The drop in borrowing costs can be chalked up to the reduction in debt. And the drop in the cost for deposits at a time when deposits increased can be attributed to the fact that the company was able to pay less in these deposits as evidenced by the fact that the weighted average yield paid out on them declined from 3.60% to 3.51%. In fact, with the exception of retail time deposits, all deposits became cheaper on a year-over-year basis. With interest rates now dropping, albeit slowly, the firm should have the opportunity to benefit. After all, assets have the potential to be locked in for long periods of time. However, deposits can be adjusted based on changing market conditions.

It is worth noting that for 2024 as a whole, Truxton Corporation generated $26 million in net interest income. This was up from the $24.2 million reported the previous year. This was partially because of the changes I already mentioned. However, during this time, the company benefited from a rise in its net interest margin from 2.67% to 2.71%. The yield on total interest-earning assets managed to climb from 5.26% to 5.76% as the company saw average balances associated with loans grow from $635.1 million to $658.2 million and the rate on those loans climb from 5.95% to 6.36%. Again, this is the benefit that banks have in an environment where interest rates are starting to drop. By comparison, there was an increase in the total cost of interest-bearing liabilities. But because of the aforementioned increase in certain assets such as loans, the net interest rate spread for the company actually improved from 1.90% to 1.95%.

Even more impressive was the rise in non-interest income from $4.3 million to $5.7 million. One contributor to this was the ‘other’ category of non-interest income that management said increased because of a large non-recurring payment from an SBIC fund that the company is invested in. Year over year for the final quarter, that rise was nearly $0.3 million. For the year as a whole, the increase amounted to $0.6 million. There were other changes as well. Most notably, the company saw a year-over-year decline in losses on securities of $0.3 million during the final quarter, plus for the year as a whole, the business saw an increase in wealth management services revenue from $17.7 million to $20.6 million.

Thanks to these improvements, net profits for the final quarter of the year came in at $5 million. That compares favorably to the $4.2 million reported the previous year. For the year as a whole, the firm generated a profit of $18.4 million. That happens to be 5.1% greater than the $17.5 million that the business reported for 2023 in its entirety. Admittedly, these are not significant changes. But the overall trend is certainly a positive for shareholders.

When it comes to valuing the company, the process is pretty straightforward. In the chart above, you can see how shares are priced relative to earnings. With a price to earnings multiple of 11.8, the stock is a bit more expensive than what I like to see. Usually, I like banks to trade at between 6 and 10 times earnings. However, as that chart also illustrates, only one of the five companies that I compared Truxton Corporation to ended up being cheaper than it is. In the chart below, I did the same type of comparative analysis using both the price to book approach and the price to tangible book approach. This is where the stock doesn't look so cheap. In fact, it is more expensive than any of these other firms.

With multiples like these, I would normally assign a ‘hold’ rating. However, this is an exception. This exception is because of the high asset quality of the institution. In the first chart below, for instance, you can see its return on assets of 1.94%. This dwarfs the return on asset readings of any of the five banks that I compared it to. And in the subsequent chart, you can see the same thing regarding return on equity. The 20.43% that we get is comfortably higher than any of these other institutions. This certainly deserves something of a premium.

Takeaway

At this time, Truxton Corporation might not be the best prospect on the market. But it is certainly a decent one. High asset quality, continued income statement growth, declining debt, and a price to earnings multiple that, while elevated, is on the low end of the spectrum compared to comparable firms, all justify a soft ‘buy’ rating in my book.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: American Century Growth Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy Highlights

Growth philosophy defined by business improvement. We seek to invest in larger U.S. companies that are demonstrating their businesses are improving, as opposed to absolute levels of growth. We believe these companies add value over time through increasing earnings growth and the ability to command a higher valuation relative to peers.

Returns driven by stock selection. We construct our portfolio to intentionally acquire stock-specific risk aligned with our research, while minimizing relative exposures related to sectors and common factor risks, such as market-cap size, volatility and momentum.

Team portfolio management by dedicated sector specialists. All members of the team conduct research and participate in portfolio decisions. This provides advantages of efficiency, alignment and accountability.

Portfolio Review

Stocks posted gains. The reelection of former President Donald Trump initially pleased investors who had worried about another protracted legal battle regarding the election's results. However, the Federal Reserve's signal that it will slow the pace of interest rate cuts amid persistent inflation largely erased postelection gains.

Growth outperformed at every capitalization range. Mid- and large-cap growth stocks led U.S. equities higher during the quarter, outperforming small-cap growth stocks, which posted more modest gains. Value stocks declined across the market-capitalization spectrum.

Information technology detracted. Stock choices in the sector hurt performance, especially in the semiconductors and semiconductor equipment industry. Software also detracted due to stock decisions.

Capital markets weighed on performance. Stock selection in the industry was negative, helping to drive weakness in the financials sector. S&P Global underperformed on investor concerns that rising long-term interest rates and comparisons against a strong 2024 will weigh on debt issuance growth rates in 2025.

Real estate benefited performance. Sector outperformance was primarily due to the specialized real estate investment trusts industry, where we did not own interest rate- sensitive names such as American Tower.

Key Contributors

Merck & Co. (MRK) The pharmaceutical company's stock lagged as Gardasil sales continue to be weak, driven by continued lower demand in China. As Merck's loss of patent protection for its blockbuster drug Keytruda approaches, weakness in Gardasil continues to be a concern. Our lack of exposure benefited performance.

Adobe (ADBE). Not owning the software company helped portfolio performance. Investors are increasingly concerned about the competitive threat of generative artificial intelligence on the software company's creative business.

Dutch Bros (BROS). The drive-through coffee chain reported much better revenue and earnings than had been expected. Dutch Bros. is adding locations and testing new menu items.

Key Detractors

Palantir Technologies (PLTR). Postelection optimism around increased defense spending buoyed the stock. A founder is close to the president-elect, and investors expect favorable deal flow under the new administration. Our lack of exposure to Palantir detracted.

Novo Nordisk (NVO). The Denmark-based pharmaceutical company underperformed as a trial for its next-generation obesity asset CagriSema disappointed investors.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). The semiconductor manufacturer reported results below expectations, which increased investor skepticism in the revenue trajectory for its artificial intelligence graphics processing unit business.

Notable Trades

Arista Networks (ANET). We established a position in this computer networking company, where we see strong long-term growth in artificial intelligence networking, driven by the adoption of Ethernet technology in larger AI clusters. We also expect robust share gains for Arista in enterprise campus, at the expense of Cisco Systems.

Apollo Global Management (APO). We initiated a position in this asset manager. We see strong long-term structural growth drivers for alternative asset managers, including increasing penetration of retail and wealth management markets and infrastructure.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). We are less optimistic on its AI graphics processing unit business, and we do not think it will have a competitive offering to NVIDIA until at least 2026. We exited our position.

Sysco (SYY). We exited our position in this food wholesaler as management's execution lagged our expected recovery of margin growth due to ongoing pressures on its restaurant customers.

Positioning for the Future

Our process uses analysis aimed at identifying large-cap companies producing attractive, sustainable earnings growth. We seek to reduce unintended risks and align the portfolio with company-specific risks that we believe will be rewarded over time. As a result of this approach, our sector and industry allocations reflect where we are finding opportunities at a given time.

There were no major sector weight changes. Our sector weightings remain fairly close to the benchmark, with all sector variation less than 1%.

We like consumer discretionary. Positioning within the specialty retail industry, specifically in off-price retailer The TJX Cos., is driving the sector overweight relative to the benchmark. We see consumers increasingly shopping for value and believe these retailers benefit from increased demand and more availability of goods from excess inventory at traditional retailers.

Artificial intelligence is helping to drive our communication services position. We are seeing AI being deployed most effectively in the digital advertising space by companies such as Alphabet and Meta Platforms, which are using AI to improve their recommendation engines that make advertisements more relevant and user content more engaging. We believe this will drive more durable revenue growth over the near to medium term. Additionally, we view Alphabet as a leader in generative AI via its development of the suite of Gemini large language models, its infrastructure with Google Cloud and its large consumer applications such as Search, Maps, Gmail and YouTube that provide data for model training.

We continue to favor health care. Pharmaceuticals and biotechnology are our largest sector overweight industries. However, we are underweight health care providers and services as health care utilization remains elevated. Pricing for Medicare programs may be weaker than expected, which could limit earnings upside.

We see opportunities in information technology. Although we are underweight the sector, it is our largest absolute position. The emergence of generative AI as a theme has sparked increased interest in technology industries such as semiconductors and software. While generative AI tools are not broadly deployed today, companies are preparing for this eventuality by investing in solutions that help them better capture and organize data. Simultaneously, the large public cloud providers are investing significant resources to grow their data center capacity as AI will need both increased data storage and advanced computational capabilities.

Goal and Strategy

Long-term capital growth by investing primarily in large U.S. companies.

The Russell 1000® Index measures the performance of the 1,000 largest companies in the Russell 3000® Index (the 3,000 largest publicly traded U.S. companies based on total market capitalization). The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 1000® companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. Created by Frank Russell Company, indices are not investment products available for purchase.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The S&P 500 Index (VOO): Will Its Historic 10.7% Return Continue?
data.description:
Does the market always go up over the long term? Most investors have come to think so. This might look like a particularly good moment to have faith in this view as investors who have ten or fifteen years in the market have seen nothing but success from buying the Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF (NYSEARCA:VOO) and then hanging on through its minor ups and downs. It's clearly in the interest of brokers and fund/ETF purveyors to sell this view to potential clients. Young investors are taught that they can pretty much depend on a 10% annualized return - 10.7% to be exact - if they start when they are young and invest regularly. As the years pass that 10% plus average annually compounded return will grow to a very large number. "Time in the market, not timing the market" is the catch phrase. There are also well researched studies that show that, yes, the market has always gone up in the long run. Read the previous line again and note the verb tense. What follows is an analysis of the complicated relationship that exists between the past and the future especially when it comes to forecasts and predictions about the financial world.

"It's Hard To Make Predictions, Especially About The Future"

The above heading is attributed to both Niels Bohr and Yogi Berra, a range of thinkers which is only strengthened by its apparent incongruity. Human beings tend to look back from the present moment and assume that the future is likely to look pretty much like the past. This tendency afflicts well-versed and thoughtful individuals as much as it afflicts less intellectual individuals who just extrapolate from their repetitive experience on a day to day basis. There's no better example of this tendency than Francis Fukuyama who in 1992 published an acclaimed book entitled The End Of History. His argument was that the Cold War had ended and liberal democracy and the market economy had prevailed decisively over all their foes. Just a decade later he began to back away from this view finally admitting when the War on Terror began after 9/11 that "history had just taken a vacation."

Fukuyama is far from the only example. Both the philosopher Hegel and the eminent historian Ranke argued in the first half of the 19th Century that the end point of history lay in the evolving preeminence of "the Prussian state." They certainly didn't nail that one. The French intellectual Raymond Aron published "Century of Total War" in 1953, which was pretty much exactly the moment when the two great World Wars began to recede from memory and were replaced by small and inconclusive wars in Korea, Vietnam, and many parts of the Middle East. Predictions of the future like the above tend mainly to show how firmly the author was anchored in the present.

The assumption that future market performance will closely resemble the past is the core underpinning of the famous 75 year study by Roger Ibbotson and Peng Chen entitled Stock Market Returns in the Long Run: Participating in the Real Economy. The study concluded that in the 75 years from 1926 to 2000 the S&P 500 Index had an average annualized return of 10.7%. The Ibbotson and Chen study has become the gold standard for backward looking studies of the market based on detailed data. Ibbotson and Chen decomposed the number 10.7% using six methods which at times drew upon data from the real world of the economy such as GDP growth and productivity. It is thus at a slight remove from the performance of the equity market itself. This should come as no surprise since the lead author was a Yale economist. Each of the six decompositions produced the same number: 10.7%. The simplest version decomposing the sources of market performance is this simple equation: Total Return = Earnings Per Share Growth + Change In The Price to Earnings Ratio + Dividends. Change in the price to earnings ratio adds 1.25% annualized to all methods of decomposing the 10.7%.

Looking backward from 2001 real earnings growth contributed about 3%, dividends contributed a little over 4%), and inflation contributed 3.5%. Their assumptions called for adding share buybacks and other investments including acquisitions as actual dividend payments have fallen over three decades to a dividend yield which today is 1.26%. Other ways of decomposing the 10.7% total include deriving earnings growth from GDP growth (bearing in mind that earnings growth can't in the long term exceed GDP), using dividend growth rather than earnings growth, or using nominal GDP which folds inflation into corporate earnings growth. If you are interested in this sort of approach you can google Ibbotson/Chen study 2002. Morningstar absorbed Ibbotson Associates in 2005 and its data been has been extended as part of Morningstar's fund analysis.

On the face of it there's nothing obviously wrong with the Ibbotson/Chen study, although the original study was written in the somewhat stiff and overly complex manner of papers written by economists. There is, however, one very important question that it does not address. Does it matter where a study like Ibbotson/Chen begins and ends? With their great attention to rigor and detail Ibbotson and Chen were clearly not much worried about that. As it happened they started at a moment which looked pretty much random: 1926. The S&P 500 did not actually exist as an index until March 1957 but Ibbotson and Chen started with 1926 explaining that it was the first year for which good data was available. In 1926 the S&P index was put together as a 90-stock index calculated daily replacing a 233 stock average established by Standard Statistics, the precursor to Standard and Poor's. Ibbotson and Chen chose to start from the more rigorous 90-stock index initiated in 1926. It so happened that 1926 was the exact middle of the great bull market which took place in the Roaring Twenties 20s but well before the final run-up to the market top on September 3, 1929.

One way of thinking about the importance of starting points for an index is to imagine how well you would have done as an investor if you had put all of your money into the index on a particular date. The short answer is that it matters a lot. On the first trading day of 1926 the S&P 500 Index was valued at 322.26 with a Price/Earnings ratio of 10.22 which rose strongly throughout the year so that the P/E on the last trading day had risen to 14.89 a P/E which was not uncommon for several decades before 1926. It's close to the 15 P/E ratio which conservative analysts often cite as the neutral P/E ratio for the market as a whole. You can look up P/E ratios going back to 1871 here. All P/Es in the chart and used in this analysis are based on trailing twelve month basis (TTM) rather than forward earnings commonly used today which are really just estimates pulled out of the air and often used to make the market and individual stocks appear cheaper than they are.

On the day of the final market top in September 1929 the S&P Index was valued at 582.26 with a P/E ratio of 19.46. Earnings were up slightly since 1926 but the P/E ratio was up 80%, a huge advance not supported by increased earnings. The Dow Jones Industrial Average peaked on September 3, 1929, at 361. After the Crash the market continued to fall until the Dow bottomed on July 8, 1932, down 89%. The S&P 90 stock index fell more than 75%. The Great Depression began in 1930 and persisted throughout the decade. If you want to look up the full series linked above it's important to bear in mind that the high P/E ratios through most of the 1930s were the result of near zero earnings through much of that decade. Here are some numbers to reflect upon:

  • If you had bought the S&P Index on day one of 1926 the value of your portfolio would have almost doubled by the 1929 top but would have fallen relentlessly to the July 1932 bottom losing 75% of its value. The Dow Jones Industrial Average, then the more common benchmark, lost 89% of its value. The S&P Index managed to tag its day one 1926 value briefly in 1937 before falling sharply. It did not finally break out above 322 until November 1954, more than 25 years after the Crash. That's quite a while to wait before beginning to get your promised 10.7% although dividends helped a bit to cut losses.
  • If you had waited and bought on July 8, 1932 you would have cleaned up. I have seen evidence of this in portfolios handed down by astute grandparents. As a teenager I looked up numbers in the wonderful S&P little gray book full of statistics and saw that most of the bluest blue chips of the 1950s could have been bought (split adjusted) for less than a dollar. That's for anyone who had any cash left to invest in 1932. Wow! It's clear that the Ibbotson/Chen 10.7% came in a lumpy fashion to say the least.

The few years around 1926 proved to be a major turning point. Before the 1920s, stocks had no real long term trend, although they had generally declined in the first two decades of the 20th century which included the often forgotten Crash of 1907 and World War I. Major stock movements were brief and as Jesse Livermore's Reminiscences of a Stock Operator reveals, speculation was the order of the day. The great J.P Morgan famously answered a question about the market's future saying simply, "Stocks will fluctuate." Common Stocks as Long Term Investments (1924), by Edgar Lawrence Smith was the first academic study to put forth the idea that companies had the potential to grow by reinvesting earnings and could be more than vehicles for speculation. Until Smith's book it was widely believed that serious investors owned stocks purely for their dividends, which sometimes were increased but were often cut. Without really intending to do so, Ibbotson and Chen had begun their study at the exact moment when the view of stocks was undergoing enormous change.

The Ibbotson Chen study might have been quite a bit different if they had found sufficient data to begin in 1901 rather than 1926. Inflation had been erratic in the decades before the 1920s and had no predictable trend so neither investors nor consumers paid much attention to it. The most significant inflation occurred as soldiers returned from World War I and it reversed quickly after a couple of years. The idea of real return, the return after deducting inflation, would not have been a factor to individuals investing in 1926. For that matter, Deflation not Inflation was the problem throughout the 1930s and approached 80%. The few people who had money were able to buy five times as much of pretty much anything as they could buy in 1929. People who had money to invest could similarly buy five times the prevailing value of a good business. Looking forward to the future nobody in the Roaring Twenties would have thought that inflation would be a persistent problem fifty years in the future, and it certainly wouldn't have occurred to them that inflation at 3.5% would ever be a major factor in several of the ways that Ibbotson and Chen decomposed market returns.

The Challenge To Ibbotson And Chen

It did not take long for a challenge to Ibbotson and Chen to emerge. After seeing pre-publication versions of the Ibbotson/Chen study, two established analysts with strong resumes joined to produce a critique published at approximately the same as Ibbotson and Chen. The senior partner, Peter Bernstein, an economist and financial historian already in his 90s, was the long time editor of The Journal of Portfolio Management and considered the top expert in institutional investing, a former OSS officer from World War II, and author of the best seller Against the Gods, The Remarkable Story of Risk. Arnott, in his late 40s, was a leading advocate of value investing, creator of fundamental indexes (based on value not market cap), and founder of Research Associates. Bernstein and Arnott cited Ibbotson and Chen with respect, but their very important differences made it clear that their paper presented something like a super-heavyweight tag team match between the top contenders.

In a paper entitled "What Risk Premium Is 'Normal'?," Bernstein and Arnott presented an argument about the difficulties in arriving at the correct equity risk premium - the higher return required by stocks because they were so much riskier than bonds. Based less on the overall economy and more on the market itself the Bernstein/Arnott article is in fact a direct critique and rebuttal of the Ibbotson/Chen view that the persistent market return should continue to return around, on average, something close to 10.7%. They argued that Ibbotson and Chen had too easily accepted many assumptions such as the notion that corporate earnings and more particularly dividends closely track GDP and the notion that the current puny 1.26% average dividend return is made up for by share buybacks plus internal corporate investments and acquisitions. Bernstein and Arnott countered that buybacks amount to only about 1% while internal investments and acquisitions rarely produce an increase in return on capital. Thus the 4% number for dividends is pretty much cut in half, taking 2% away from the expected 10.7% return. They stress the fact that GDP growth has not in recent history been close to 3% and seems stuck at present well under 2%. The 1.25% average annual increase in valuation is described as one of the most dubious elements of the 10.7% as it is by its nature unlikely to repeat rise in valuation from the 25 price to its present level 70 price to yield. In the more common usage, the decline from 4% yield to the 1.4% yield at that time of their writing was unlikely to recur. If anything the 1.25% annualized increase in valuation is likely to run in reverse.

In effect, Bernstein and Arnott are saying that investors should think twice before accepting the idea that in the long run the market index promises anything like a 10.7% return in the future. Ibbotson had been the senior author of a paper presenting much of that view in 1976, and over the 25 years that followed it had been happily embraced by sell side analysts. The promise of a return in excess of 10% had proved seductive to investors and much of what happened over the next two and a half decades seemed to reinforce that view. No one troubled to think through the fact that the years from 1982 to 2000 were an exceptional period which contained almost all the P/E ratio increase used in the Ibbotson and Chen study as stocks rose in value as the rate on Treasury bonds fell from an all time high around 16%. The arguments of Bernstein and Arnott seemed heretical because they threatened to undermine the comfortable view that in the long run index investors could always count on getting returns of at least 10%.

Bernstein and Arnott described the performance of the American market since 1926 as consisting in large part of survivor bias - the fact that in the full century from 1901 to 2001 the United States had not suffered any of the great destructors including war on its own territory, revolution, political instability, government confiscation of capital, and runaway inflation. In fact, the United States started the period around 1920 as more or less an advanced emerging market similar to Argentina which at the same time (1890-1930) had the highest per capita income of any country thanks to exports of beef and other commodities. It all fell apart for Argentina which by the end of the global Great Depression was in bad economic straits which enabled a socialist party to come to power. Eventually Argentina saw its stock market go to zero, one of the four countries which did so in the 20th Century (the others being Russia, China, and Egypt). Two other countries came close, Japan and Germany (twice).

Meanwhile the United States benefitted from winning its wars (OK, Bill Murray in Stripes said we went 10 and 1, the 1 being the war I participated in). This led to global dominance with the world's reserve currency and well regulated financial markets, along with outstanding capacity for innovation. The U.S. thus ranks second in real annualized returns of all of all stock markets for the 100 years from 1901 through 2000 . The #1 country in average real returns in the 20th century was, of course, Sweden. Nobody has yet answered this question correctly, but it makes perfect sense as Sweden had stayed out of both World Wars as well as lesser conflicts, enjoyed stable government with rational economic policies, and was able to sell raw materials to both sides during a global war. The annualized 100-year real return for the Swedish market was 8.2% versus 6.9% for the U.S. (All data is from Millennium Book II by Dimson, March, and Staunton.) In the past quarter century, however, the U.S. has pulled ahead.

To be sure, I am not forecasting catastrophes in the future of the United States. It is entirely possible that there is a grain of truth in the assertion of American exceptionalism. What is inescapable, however, is that such a period or uninterrupted progress cannot be repeated. The rise from an emerging market to the world's economic and military superpower can happen only once. The Ibbotson/Chen paper makes no effort to take that into account but stands firmly in the present moment while attempting to extend the past into the future.

Bernstein and Arnott have the better argument, and because they published in 2002 it's not a matter of conjecture. Arnott implied in that paper and said outright in another that the equity risk premium was probably less than zero at that moment in time. That meant that over a fairly long period, Treasury bonds were likely to perform better than stocks as measured by the S&P 500 Index. We don't have to wait on that one. The S&P 500 Index had a negative return of 0.9% for the entire decade between 2001 and 2010. Bonds performed much better. As Arnott argued in two separate papers, the equity risk premium had started that decade at less than zero.

Conclusion

On October 18, 2024, Goldman Sachs presented a prediction that for the next 10 years, the S&P 500 would have at best an annualized return of only 3% for a 10-year cumulative return of about 34%. Goldman's high status made those numbers turn heads, but the arguments behind them were largely presented with broad brush concerns about current valuation, exceptional concentration of the market, and the likelihood of more negative economic events. They further suggested that bonds might outperform stocks over the coming ten years - suggesting in Bernstein and Arnott terms, a "negative risk premium" for owning stocks. It happened that I had broadly the same view as Goldman. Investors enthusiastic about AI and the successes of high tech growth stocks over the previous decade skimmed the Goldman articles and shrugged.

My own response was to reread the earlier and more rigorous articles about equity risk premium and reasonable expectations for future returns presented in the above papers. The details may be overwhelming to some readers, but some detailed knowledge of the arguments should be very helpful to most investors and at least make it harder to dismiss forecasts like Goldman's with a shrug. Both God and the devil are in the details, and the above details point clearly in the direction that Bernstein and Arnott were the actual winners of the super-heavyweight bout in 2002. They presented better and more detailed arguments than the Ibbotson and Chen expected return of 10.7%, however strongly many investors would like to believe it.

The most powerful macro argument was that the last 100 years of American history were exceptional for the great good fortune of avoiding war on our own territory, avoiding political instability, having no revolutions or government confiscation of capital, and no persistent damaging inflation (the 1970s perhaps came close). We weren't Argentina, Russia, China, or Egypt, nor were we like Germany or Japan. Although Sweden's market won the 20th Century for many of the same reasons, we had pulled ahead by 2025, thanks to American entrepreneurship and innovation, important because the life expectancy of an individual company is approximately 18 years. Entrepreneurship and innovation are much more powerful in the U.S. than in Europe. We won because we were the survivors. Perhaps we are exceptional. It's certain that we were also lucky.

The success of the Ibbotson/Chen study had a lot to do with the good luck in their starting point and the fortunate ground they stood on when they did their study. The market PE on day one of 1926 was 10.22 but had risen to 25.96 on the last day of 2001. Even if we continue to have good luck it is highly unlikely that the P/E ratio of American stocks will again multiply two and a half times and reach 64. Nvidia (NVDA) maybe. The American market as a whole, no way. From its current 28.77 trailing twelve month P/E it is far more likely that the overall market P/E will decline, probably as much as 50%, the amount needed to bring it in line with the long term standard of 15. That would dock the future return projection by 2.5%, added to the 2% subtracted from the dividend/buyback number, and the 1 to 1.5% subtracted from current and recent GDP growth. That takes the nominal expected return down to 2% or lower (Ibbotson/Chen's number for the risk premium over bonds being an arithmetic average of 6% or a geometric average of 4%).

The core problem for Ibbotson and Chen was the same as that of Fukuyama - forgetting that extrapolating the future from the past almost always implies merely being anchored too firmly in the present. Ibbotson and Chen did not fully grasp how trapped we were in the present moment and how powerfully this end point for their study distorts its results and damages their ability to have a clear view of the future.

It took just ten years for it to be clear that Bernstein and Arnott were the winners of the debate. A ten-year period is long enough and is about as far into the future as we can say anything sensible about. Bernstein and Arnott nailed it. The 2000-2009 period is now often referred to as "the lost decade" and considered an "outlier." Every good statistician knows that there is no such thing. The troubling reality is that the present moment bears so much resemblance to the year 2000. Maybe parts of the market aren't quite as expensive. Maybe the projected future of AI and the large tech stocks is not as silly and lacking in substance as the dot.com period. AI may well be the future but for which companies and what level of profits? There may even be a case to be made for a continuation of the current bull market, but it is likely that any gains from the present moment will ultimately be given back, and then some.

It does not make me happy to be the bearer of bad news. I provided the detailed argument in this article in the effort to give real substance to the Goldman view in hopes that readers can draw upon serious data in thinking about the future of the market. I am still working out the most rational thing to do with most of the stocks in my portfolio which are great companies but now consist of 60-80% embedded capital gains. Should I sell and pay the capital gains taxes? It's a very tough decision. It's even harder to know how to advise younger investors. One approach is dollar-cost-averaging into the index. Another is to own some bonds. My strategy at age 80 will likely differ from yours. Just don't act without thinking it through. Read this article from start to finish two or three times and let the facts sink in. Ask questions in your comments and I promise to answer to the best of my ability. And don't forget the cautionary advice of Niels Bohr and Yogi Berra.
